title: Git Workflow Best Practices
description: How poor Git practices cause merge conflicts, lost code, and deployment issues plus proven workflows for managing branches, commits, and collaboration effectively.
content:
# Git Workflow
Best
Practices On an early project, our team committed directly to main branch without reviews. One developer pushed breaking changes at 5pm on Friday, and we spent the weekend rolling back commits and fixing production. That's when I learned Git workflow isn't just about version control it's about protecting production and enabling collaboration. --- ##
The Problem:
Chaotic Git Usage Creates Production Issues Teams without Git workflow standards face constant problems merge conflicts, lost work, broken deployments, and unclear history. Developers commit directly to main, push untested code, and create confusing commit messages. ```bash # ❌ Bad practices git commit -m "fix" git commit -m "updates" git push origin main # Directly to production branch ``` **The consequence:** Production breaks because untested code went straight to main. Teams can't identify what changed or who changed it. Merge conflicts become daily battles. --- ## Why This Needs to Be Solved Poor Git practices cause deployment failures, lost productivity, and team friction. Without clear workflows, collaboration becomes chaotic and risky. On one project, lack of Git discipline caused 8 production rollbacks in a month. After implementing proper workflow, rollbacks dropped to zero. --- ##
The Solution:
Feature Branch Workflow ### 1. Never Commit Directly to Main Main branch should always be production-ready. All development happens in feature branches: ```bash # ✅ Create feature branch git checkout -b feature/add-user-profile # Make changes and commit git add . git commit -m "Add user profile component with avatar upload" # Push to remote git push origin feature/add-user-profile ``` **Rule:** Main only receives code through pull requests after review. --- ### 2. Use Descriptive Branch Names Branch names should indicate the work being done: ```bash # ✅ Good - Clear purpose feature/payment-integration fix/login-validation-bug refactor/api-error-handling # ❌ Bad - Vague new-stuff updates fix-123 ``` **Pattern:** `type/description` where type is feature, fix, refactor, or hotfix. --- ### 3. Write Meaningful Commit Messages Commits should explain what and why, not just what: ```bash # ✅ Good - Explains purpose git commit -m "Fix Safari payment form validation Safari doesn't support HTML5 validation for custom inputs. Added manual validation logic for payment fields." # ❌ Bad - No context git commit -m "fix bug" ``` **Format:** First line summary, blank line, detailed explanation if needed. --- ### 4. Keep Commits Focused and Atomic Each commit should represent one logical change: ```bash # ✅ Good - Separate commits git commit -m "Add user authentication API endpoint" git commit -m "Create login form component" git commit -m "Add authentication tests" # ❌ Bad - Everything in one commit git commit -m "Add login feature" # (includes API, UI, tests, config changes) ``` **Benefit:** Easy to review, easy to revert if needed. --- ### 5. Pull Before Push Always sync with remote before pushing: ```bash # ✅ Good workflow git checkout main git pull origin main git checkout feature/my-feature git merge main # Resolve conflicts locally git push origin feature/my-feature # ❌ Bad - Push without pulling git push origin feature/my-feature # Might conflict with others' work ``` --- ### 6. Use Pull Requests for All Changes Never merge directly. Always create PRs: ```bash # After pushing branch, create PR on GitHub/GitLab # Request reviews from team members # Merge only after approval and CI passes ``` **Benefits:** Code review, automated testing, discussion, documentation. --- ### 7. Delete Merged Branches Keep repository clean by removing merged branches: ```bash # After PR is merged git branch -d feature/my-feature # Delete local git push origin --delete feature/my-feature # Delete remote ``` --- ### 8. Use .gitignore Properly Never commit environment files, dependencies, or build artifacts: ```bash # .gitignore node_modules/ .env .env.local dist/ build/ .DS_Store ``` --- ## Git Flow for Team Workflow Our team uses this flow: 1. **Pull latest main:** `git pull origin main` 2. **Create feature branch:** `git checkout -b feature/name` 3. **Make changes and commit:** Focused, descriptive commits 4. **Push branch:** `git push origin feature/name` 5. **Create pull request:** Request reviews 6. **Address feedback:** Make changes, push updates 7. **Merge after approval:** Squash or merge commits 8. **Delete branch:** Clean up after merge --- ## Measured Impact After implementing Git workflow on a team of 5: **Before:** - Production rollbacks: 8/month - Merge conflicts: 15/week - Time lost to Git issues: 6 hours/week **After:** - Production rollbacks: 0/month - Merge conflicts: 2/week - Time lost to Git issues: 30 minutes/week --- ## Key Takeaways Proper Git workflow prevents production issues, reduces conflicts, and enables effective collaboration. Never commit directly to main use feature branches and pull requests. Write descriptive commit messages, keep commits atomic, and always pull before pushing. Use code reviews through pull requests for all changes. These practices protect production, improve code quality, and save hours of debugging and conflict resolution.

title: Image Optimization in Next.js: Reducing Load Time and Improving Core Web Vitals
description: How Next.js Image component solves common image performance problems like large file sizes, layout shift, and slow LCP through automatic optimization and lazy loading.
content:
# Image
Optimization
in Next.js On an early project, I used regular `` tags for a product gallery with 20 high-resolution images. The homepage took 8 seconds to load on 3G connections, and our Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) was 5.2 seconds far above Google's 2.5-second threshold. Users were abandoning the page before seeing any products. Images are typically the heaviest assets on web pages, often accounting for 50-70% of total page weight. Next.js provides a built-in Image component that solves this problem, but only if you understand how to use it properly. --- ##
The Problem:
Unoptimized Images Hurt Performance Using standard HTML `` tags in modern web applications creates multiple performance issues that developers often don't notice in development but users experience in production. Here's what happens with basic image implementation: ```javascript export default function ProductCard({ product }) { return (

{product.name}

); } ``` **What goes wrong:** **Large file sizes:** Images are served in their original format and resolution. A 3MB PNG meant for print gets sent to mobile users who only need a 200KB version. **No format optimization:** Modern formats like WebP and AVIF offer 25-35% better compression than JPEG, but browsers receive old formats because you're not converting them. **Layout shift:** Images load after the page renders, causing content to jump around. This destroys your Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) score. **All images load immediately:** Every image on the page downloads on initial load, even those below the fold that users might never see. On a recent project, the homepage had 15 product images totaling 12MB. On mobile 3G, that's 40+ seconds of download time. --- ## Why This Needs to Be Solved Unoptimized images create three critical issues that directly impact business metrics: **Poor Core Web Vitals:** Large images increase LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) and cause layout shift (CLS). Google uses these metrics for search rankings poor scores mean lower visibility. **High bounce rates:** Users abandon pages that take more than 3 seconds to load. Every second of delay can reduce conversions by 7%. **Wasted bandwidth and costs:** Serving 3MB images to mobile users wastes their data plans and increases your CDN costs. On high-traffic sites, this translates to thousands of dollars monthly. On one e-commerce project, unoptimized images were responsible for 68% of the total page weight and caused 4.8-second LCP times. --- ##
The Solution:
Next.js Image Component Next.js provides an Image component that automatically optimizes images on-demand. It handles format conversion, resizing, lazy loading, and prevents layout shift all without manual configuration. ### Basic Implementation Replace `` tags with Next.js Image component: ```javascript import Image from "next/image"; export default function ProductCard({ product }) { return (

{product.name}

); } ``` **What this solves:** **Automatic format conversion:** Next.js serves WebP or AVIF to browsers that support them, reducing file size by 25-35% without quality loss. **Responsive sizing:** The image is automatically resized based on the device screen size. Mobile users get smaller files than desktop users. **Lazy loading by default:** Images below the fold don't load until users scroll near them, reducing initial page weight. **Layout shift prevention:** Specifying width and height reserves space before the image loads, preventing content from jumping. --- ### Priority Loading for Hero Images For above-the-fold images like hero banners, lazy loading hurts performance. Use the `priority` prop to preload these critical images: ```javascript export default function Hero() { return ( ); } ``` **Why this matters:** The `priority` prop tells Next.js this image is critical for LCP. It gets preloaded in the HTML ``, ensuring it loads as quickly as possible. **Result:** On one project, adding `priority` to the hero image reduced LCP from 3.8s to 1.6s. --- ### Responsive Images with sizes Prop The `sizes` prop tells Next.js what size image to serve at different screen widths: ```javascript ``` **How this works:** - On mobile (≤768px): Serve image at 100% viewport width - On tablet (≤1200px): Serve image at 50% viewport width - On desktop (>1200px): Serve image at 33% viewport width **The benefit:** Mobile users downloading a product page get 400px images instead of 1200px images, reducing file size by 65-70%. --- ### Remote Images with Domain Configuration For images hosted on external domains (CDNs, cloud storage), configure allowed domains: ```javascript // next.config.js module.exports = { images: { remotePatterns: [ { protocol: "https", hostname: "cdn.example.com", }, { protocol: "https", hostname: "images.unsplash.com", }, ], }, }; ``` Then use remote images normally: ```javascript ``` **Why configuration is required:** Next.js protects against malicious image URLs by requiring explicit domain allowlisting. --- ### Fill Layout for Dynamic Containers When you don't know exact dimensions or want images to fill a container: ```javascript
``` **Important:** The parent container must have `position: relative` and defined dimensions. The `fill` prop makes the image absolutely positioned to fill its parent. --- ## Quality and Format Configuration Control image quality globally or per-image: ```javascript // Global configuration in next.config.js module.exports = { images: { formats: ['image/avif', 'image/webp'], // Try AVIF first, fallback to WebP minimumCacheTTL: 60, }, }; // Per-image quality ``` **Quality guidelines:** - Hero images: 85-90 - Product images: 80-85 - Thumbnails: 70-75 - Background images: 70-80 --- ## Common Mistakes and Solutions **Mistake #1: Not specifying width and height** ```javascript // ❌ Bad - Causes layout shift // ✅ Good - Prevents layout shift ``` **Mistake #2: Using priority on all images** The `priority` prop should only be used for above-the-fold images. Using it on many images defeats lazy loading and increases initial page weight. ```javascript // ❌ Bad - Too many priority images // ✅ Good - Only critical above-fold image {/* Lazy loads */} {/* Lazy loads */} ``` **Mistake #3: Not using sizes prop for responsive images** Without the `sizes` prop, Next.js can't optimize image delivery for different screen sizes. ```javascript // ❌ Bad - Same size for all devices // ✅ Good - Responsive sizing ``` **Mistake #4: Setting quality too high** Quality above 85 provides diminishing returns while significantly increasing file size. ```javascript // ❌ Bad - Unnecessary quality, larger files // ✅ Good - Optimal balance ``` --- ## Measured Impact On a production e-commerce website with 20 product images: **Before Next.js Image optimization:** - Total image size: 12.4MB - LCP: 5.2s - CLS: 0.24 - Lighthouse Performance: 52 **After Next.js Image optimization:** - Total image size: 2.1MB (83% reduction) - LCP: 1.8s - CLS: 0.02 - Lighthouse Performance: 91 **Additional benefits:** - Mobile data usage reduced by 80% - Page load time on 3G: 8s → 2.4s - CDN costs reduced by 70% --- ## Key Takeaways Images are typically the largest assets on web pages and directly impact Core Web Vitals and user experience. Unoptimized images cause slow LCP, layout shift, and high bounce rates. Next.js Image component automatically optimizes images through format conversion, responsive sizing, lazy loading, and layout shift prevention. Use it for all images instead of HTML `` tags. Always specify width and height to prevent layout shift. Use `priority` prop only for above-the-fold critical images. Configure `sizes` prop for responsive images to serve appropriate dimensions per device. Proper image optimization can reduce total page weight by 60-80% and improve Lighthouse scores by 30-40 points, directly impacting search rankings and conversion rates.

title: Folder Structure That Scales: React Edition
description: Stop reorganizing your project every month. Learn folder structures that grow with your team from startup to enterprise.
content:
# Folder Structure That Scales: React Edition Every new developer joins your project and asks, "Where do I put this component?" If you hesitate to answer, your folder structure has failed. Here's how to organize React projects that scale from 1 to 100 developers. --- ##
The Problem:
Chaotic Growth It starts simple: a `components` folder with everything. Then you hit 50 components. Then 100. Finding anything becomes a scavenger hunt. ``` src/ ├── components/ // 87 files, good luck ├── utils/ // random helpers ├── hooks/ // some of them └── pages/ // actually contains components too ``` --- ## Option 1: Feature-Based Structure (Recommended) Group by feature, not file type: ``` src/ ├── features/ │ ├── auth/ │ │ ├── components/ │ │ ├── hooks/ │ │ ├── types/ │ │ └── index.ts │ ├── products/ │ │ ├── components/ │ │ ├── hooks/ │ │ ├── api/ │ │ └── index.ts │ └── cart/ │ ├── components/ │ ├── store/ │ └── index.ts ├── shared/ │ ├── ui/ // Reusable buttons, inputs │ ├── layout/ // Headers, footers │ └── utils/ // Truly global helpers └── app/ ├── routes/ └── providers/ ``` **Why it works:** When working on auth, everything you need is in one place. No jumping between 7 folders. --- ## Option 2: Domain-Driven Design For complex domains, mirror your business logic: ``` src/ ├── domains/ │ ├── catalog/ │ │ ├── product/ │ │ ├── category/ │ │ └── search/ │ ├── ordering/ │ │ ├── cart/ │ │ ├── checkout/ │ │ └── payment/ │ └── customer/ │ ├── profile/ │ ├── orders/ │ └── wishlist/ └── shared/ ``` --- ## Option 3: Module Federation Ready For micro-frontends, isolate by module: ``` packages/ ├── shell/ // Host application ├── auth-module/ // Remote ├── products-module/ // Remote ├── checkout-module/ // Remote └── shared-ui/ // Shared components ``` --- ## The Barrel File Pattern Each feature exports its public API through `index.ts`: ```typescript // features/products/index.ts export { ProductCard } from "./components/ProductCard"; export { useProducts } from "./hooks/useProducts"; export type { Product } from "./types"; ``` Now imports are clean and intentional: ```typescript // ✅ Clear where things come from import { ProductCard, useProducts } from "@/features/products"; // ❌ Don't reach inside import ProductCard from "@/features/products/components/ProductCard/ProductCard"; ``` --- ## What Goes Where | Type | Location | Example | | ---------------- | -------------- | ------------------------------------ | | Feature-specific | Inside feature | `features/auth/components/LoginForm` | | Reusable UI | Shared/ui | `shared/ui/Button` | | Global state | Feature store | `features/cart/store` | | API calls | Feature api | `features/products/api` | | Types | Feature types | `features/auth/types` | --- ## When to Reorganize **Signs you need a better structure:** - You create files in wrong folders - Imports like `../../../components/Button` - Multiple people editing same files simultaneously - Onboarding takes days instead of hours --- ## Key Takeaways 1. **Group by feature, not file type** everything related stays together 2. **Use barrel files** for clean, intentional imports 3. **Keep shared code truly shared** don't mix feature code 4. **Be consistent** choose one pattern and document it The best structure is one your team actually follows. Pick one, stick with it, and revisit every six months.

title: React Performance: Measuring What Matters
description: Stop guessing which optimizations work. Learn to measure, identify real bottlenecks, and fix what actually impacts user experience.
content:
# React Performance: Measuring What Matters Every week, someone adds useMemo and useCallback everywhere "just in case." Every month, a team wastes hours optimizing renders that don't matter. Here's the truth: **You can't fix what you don't measure.** --- ##
The Problem:
Premature Optimization Developers guess at performance issues. "This component feels slow let's memoize everything." But without data, you're just rearranging deck chairs. --- ## Step 1: Measure With React DevTools Profiler The Profiler tab tells you exactly what's happening: ```typescript // Flag commits that take too long { if (actualTime > 16) { // 16ms = 60fps target console.warn(`Slow render: ${id} took ${actualTime}ms`); } }}> ``` **What to look for:** - Commits taking longer than 16ms - Components that render when props haven't changed - Render cascades (one component causing many to render) --- ## Step 2: Why Did You Render? The `why-did-you-render` library catches unnecessary renders: ```typescript import whyDidYouRender from '@welldone-software/why-did-you-render'; if (process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development') { whyDidYouRender(React, { trackAllPureComponents: true, trackHooks: true, logOnDifferentValues: true }); } // Mark components to track const ExpensiveComponent = React.memo(({ data }) => { return
{/* complex UI */}
; }); ExpensiveComponent.whyDidYouRender = true; ``` Now your console shows exactly why a component re-rendered and whether you can prevent it. --- ## Step 3: Browser Performance Tab React DevTools shows component renders. Browser Performance tab shows the full picture: **What to check:** - Long tasks (>50ms) blocking the main thread - Forced reflows/layout thrashing - Large JavaScript parse times --- ## Step 4: Core Web Vitals Real user metrics matter most: ```typescript // Report to your analytics import { onCLS, onFID, onLCP } from 'web-vitals'; function sendToAnalytics(metric) { const body = JSON.stringify(metric); navigator.sendBeacon('/analytics', body); } onCLS(sendToAnalytics); // Layout shifts onFID(sendToAnalytics); // Input delay onLCP(sendToAnalytics); // Loading speed ``` --- ## Common Bottlenecks (With Evidence) **1. Large lists without virtualization** - Measure: Render time grows with list size - Fix: React Window or Virtualized **2. Unnecessary context updates** - Measure: Entire tree re-rendering on state change - Fix: Split contexts, use selectors **3. Expensive calculations on every render** - Measure: Component takes >16ms consistently - Fix: useMemo for heavy computations --- ## Case Study: E-commerce Product Page **Before measuring:** - Team wanted to memoize all components - Estimated 2 weeks of work **After measuring:** - Profiler showed one expensive image gallery re-rendering - Fixed in 2 hours with proper key and useMemo - Render time dropped from 45ms to 12ms --- ## What Actually Needs Optimization | Scenario | Action | Impact | |----------|--------|--------| | Component >16ms in Profiler | Memoize or restructure | High | | Many components re-rendering unnecessarily | Fix dependencies | High | | "feels slow" but Profiler shows <16ms | Check network/backend | Medium | | Just adding memo everywhere | Stop | Negative | --- ## Key Takeaways 1. **Always measure first** React DevTools Profiler is your friend 2. **Target 60fps** 16ms per frame or less 3. **Fix real bottlenecks**, not imaginary ones 4. **Monitor real users** with Core Web Vitals The best optimization is the one you have data for. Everything else is just guessing.

title: The Strategic Approach to Lighthouse Performance: Achieving and Maintaining 90+ Scores
description: A systematic methodology for optimizing web performance, from understanding Core Web Vitals to maintaining 90+ Lighthouse scores in production environments.
content:
# The Strategic Approach to Lighthouse Performance: Achieving and Maintaining 90+ Scores When I first started working on a large-scale client project, I thought achieving high Lighthouse scores was just about making things "fast." I was wrong. It's about understanding why performance matters and systematically addressing each bottleneck. After optimizing 10+ production websites and consistently hitting 90+ scores, I've learned that performance optimization isn't magic it's a methodical process. Let me walk you through my practical approach. --- ## Why Lighthouse Scores Actually Matter Lighthouse scores aren't just vanity metrics they directly impact three critical areas: **User Experience:** A one-second delay in page load can reduce conversions by 7%. When users wait, they leave. **SEO Rankings:** Google uses Core Web Vitals as a ranking factor. Better performance means better visibility. **Business Impact:** On one project, performance optimizations contributed to a 170% increase in conversions. Speed literally translates to revenue. --- ## The Three Performance Categories Lighthouse measures performance across three main areas. When your score is low, the problem always falls into one of these categories: 1. **Rendering Performance** (LCP, CLS) - How quickly your page becomes visually complete and stable 2. **Network Efficiency** - How fast users download your resources 3. **Code Execution** (TBT, FID) - How quickly JavaScript runs and responds to interactions --- ## My 5-Step Optimization Checklist ### Step 1: Fix Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) LCP measures how long it takes for the largest visible element to load. Google wants this under 2.5 seconds. **The mistake I made:** I'd optimize everything except the hero image the most important element users see first. **The solution:** Priority loading for critical images and optimized font loading. ```javascript import Image from "next/image"; import { Inter } from "next/font/google"; const inter = Inter({ subsets: ["latin"], display: "swap", // Shows fallback font while loading }); export default function Hero() { return ( ); } ``` **Why this works:** The `priority` prop preloads the hero image immediately, and `display: 'swap'` shows text with a fallback font instantly no invisible text while fonts load. --- ### Step 2: Eliminate Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) CLS measures visual stability. When elements jump around during page load, it creates a poor user experience. **My early mistake:** Not reserving space for images and dynamic content. Pages would jump every time an image loaded. **The solution:** Always specify dimensions and use skeletons for loading states. ```javascript // Always specify exact image dimensions ; // Reserve space for dynamic content export default function ProductCard({ product, isLoading }) { if (isLoading) { return
; } return
{product.name}
; } ``` **Why this matters:** Users don't have to re-read content because it shifted. The page feels stable and professional. --- ### Step 3: Optimize JavaScript Execution Total Blocking Time (TBT) measures how quickly your page responds to user interactions. Heavy JavaScript blocks the main thread. **What I learned the hard way:** Importing entire libraries just to use one function adds hundreds of kilobytes of unnecessary code. **The solution:** Code splitting and tree-shaking. ```javascript // Dynamic imports - only load when needed const handleOpenModal = async () => { const { Modal } = await import("./Modal"); setShowModal(true); }; // ❌ Bad: Imports entire library (71KB) import _ from "lodash"; // ✅ Good: Imports only what you need (~2KB) import debounce from "lodash/debounce"; ``` **Why this works:** Smaller JavaScript bundles mean faster parse and execution times. The page becomes interactive much quicker. --- ### Step 4: Optimize Images and Resources Efficient resource loading is critical. Every oversized image slows down your site. **The solution:** Use Next.js Image component with proper compression and modern formats. ```javascript ``` **Pro tip:** Next.js automatically converts images to WebP, which provides 25-35% better compression than JPEG without quality loss. --- ### Step 5: Monitor Continuously Achieving a good score once is easy. Maintaining it requires monitoring. **Tools I use:** - **Lighthouse CI** in GitHub Actions - Automatically tests every pull request - **Vercel Analytics** - Monitors real user metrics in production - **Chrome DevTools** - For deep debugging when issues arise ```yaml # .github/workflows/lighthouse.yml name: Lighthouse CI on: [pull_request] jobs: lighthouse: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v2 - name: Run Lighthouse uses: treosh/lighthouse-ci-action@v9 with: urls: "https://staging.example.com" ``` --- ## Real Results from a Recent E-commerce Project **Before optimization:** - Performance: 62 - LCP: 4.2s - CLS: 0.18 - TBT: 890ms **After optimization:** - Performance: 94 - LCP: 1.8s - CLS: 0.02 - TBT: 180ms **What made the biggest difference:** 1. Hero image optimization (reduced LCP by 1.8s) 2. Font optimization (eliminated CLS) 3. Code splitting (reduced TBT by 710ms) --- ## Common Mistakes to Avoid **Mistake #1:** Optimizing everything at once. Fix one metric at a time and measure the impact. **Mistake #2:** Ignoring mobile performance. Always test on mobile most users browse on phones. **Mistake #3:** Over-optimizing at the expense of UX. Balance is key use tools like GSAP efficiently rather than removing interactions entirely. **Mistake #4:** Not testing in production conditions. Test with throttled networks (Slow 3G) to simulate real-world scenarios. --- ## Key Takeaways Performance optimization is systematic follow the three categories: rendering, network, and code execution. Focus on user-centric metrics like LCP, CLS, and FID because they directly correlate with user experience. Optimize incrementally by changing one thing, measuring the impact, then moving to the next. Set up automated testing to catch regressions early, but remember to balance performance with functionality. The goal isn't just hitting 100 it's creating a fast, delightful user experience that users love and businesses value. ---

title: How Closures Really Work in JavaScript?
description: Understanding the closures behaviour in various condition, how important is it?
content:
# How Closures Really Work in JavaScript?

We don’t struggle with writing closures,

we struggle with **understanding how they work.**

You’ve probably seen this:

```js
function outer() {
  let count = 0;
  return function inner() {
    count++;
    console.log(count);
  };
}
const fn = outer();
fn(); // 1
fn(); // 2
```

Why does `count` not disappear?

`outer()` has already finished execution.  
So why does `inner()` still remember `count`?

To understand closures properly, we must use the **Execution Context model**, not memorized rules.

---

## First: What Is a Closure?

A closure is created when:

> A function remembers variables from its lexical scope even after the outer function has finished executing.

But that definition alone doesn’t explain _how_.

Let’s break it down structurally.

---

## Step 1: Execution Context Is Created

When `outer()` runs:

const fn = outer();

JavaScript:

1.  Creates a new Function Execution Context for `outer`
2.  Allocates memory for `count`
3.  Stores `inner` function in memory
4.  Returns `inner`

Important part:

The returned function (`inner`) still holds a reference to its **lexical environment**.

---

## Step 2: What Actually Gets Preserved?

Inside the engine:

- `outer` has its own **Lexical Environment**
- That environment contains:
  - `count`
  - reference to outer scope

When `outer` returns, its execution context is removed from the Call Stack.

But its **lexical environment is not garbage collected**.

Why?

Because `inner` still references it.

This is the closure.

---

## Think of It Like This

Execution Context disappears.  
Lexical Environment may survive.

The function carries a hidden reference to:

```
[[Environment]] → outer's memory
```

As long as something references that environment, it stays in memory.

---

## Visual Mental Model

When `outer()` runs:

```
Call Stack:
Global()
 → outer()

Memory Heap:
count → 0
inner → function (with reference to outer scope)
```

When `outer()` finishes:

```
Call Stack:
Global()

Memory Heap:
count → still accessible (because inner references it)
```

That reference is the closure.

---

## Why Closures Matter

Closures enable:

- Data privacy
- Function factories
- Memoization
- State in functional programming
- React hooks (internally rely on closure behavior)

---

## Example: Data Privacy

```
function createCounter() {
 let count = 0;
return {
 increment() {
  count++;
 },
 get() {
  return count;
 }
 };
}
const counter = createCounter();
counter.increment();
console.log(counter.get()); // 1
```

Here, `count` is:

- Not global
- Not directly accessible
- Still preserved

This is closure used intentionally.

---

## Common Interview Trap

```
for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
 setTimeout(() => {
  console.log(i);
 }, 100);
}

Output:
3
3
3
```

Why?

Because:

- `var` is function-scoped
- All callbacks share the same lexical environment
- Loop completes → `i` becomes 3
- All closures reference the same variable

Now with `let`:

```
for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
 setTimeout(() => {
 console.log(i);
 }, 100);
}

Output:
0
1
2
```

Because `let` creates a new block scope per iteration.

---

## The Core Truth About Closures

Closures are not special syntax.

They are a **natural consequence of:**

- Lexical scoping
- Execution contexts
- Persistent lexical environments
- Garbage collection rules

If a function references a variable, that variable cannot be cleaned up.

That’s all a closure is.

---

## Final Mental Model

When you return a function from another function:

1.  A lexical environment is created
2.  The inner function keeps a reference to it
3.  Even after the outer function exits
4.  That environment survives in memory

Closures are memory + scope + references.

Not magic.

title: How JavaScript Actually Executes Code
description: A deep dive into execution context, call stack, and scope in JavaScript.
content:
# How JavaScript
Actually
Executes
Code You log something to the console… and the output is not what you expected. - A variable is undefined even though you declared it. - A setTimeout runs after a Promise. - A function works before it’s defined. At first, it feels inconsistent. But it’s not random. JavaScript is actually following a very specific execution process. To understand why these “weird” behaviors happen, we need to understand how JavaScript actually executes code. And it all starts with something called the execution cycle. --- ## The JavaScript Execution Cycle ![Image](https://miro.medium.com/1%2AeFvdHDgxCM6C20Zt80I7Jg.png) At a high level, every JavaScript program goes through: 1. **Parsing** 2. **Execution Context Creation** 3. **Creation Phase (Memory Setup)** 4. **Execution Phase (Code Runs)** 5. **Call Stack Handling** 6. **Event Loop Processing (for async)** Now let’s simplify each stage. --- ## Stage One: Parsing Before any code runs, the engine: - Reads the entire file - Converts it into an internal structure (AST) - Checks for syntax errors If there’s a syntax error, execution never starts. --- ## Stage Two: Execution Context Creation When your file starts running, JavaScript creates something called the: ### Global Execution Context (GEC) This is the base environment where: - Global variables live - Functions are stored - `this` is defined This context is pushed onto the **Call Stack** first. --- ## Stage Three: Creation Phase (Where Hoisting Happens) Before executing code line by line, JavaScript prepares memory. Example: ```js console.log(x); var x = 10; ``` During Creation Phase: ``` x → undefined ``` During Execution Phase: ``` console.log(x); // undefined x = 10; ``` That is hoisting. --- ### What About `let` and `const`? ```js console.log(a); let a = 5; ``` This throws: ``` ReferenceError ``` Because `let` and `const` exist in a **Temporal Dead Zone** until initialized. --- ## Stage Four: Execution Phase Now JavaScript executes code line by line. ```js var x = 10; x = 20; ``` Memory changes from: ``` x → undefined ``` to: ``` x → 10 → 20 ``` If a function is called, a **new execution context** is created. --- ## The Call Stack ![Image](https://youshaohua.com/assets/img/post/simply-and-easily-understanding-function-call-stack-by-a-vision/thum.jpg) The Call Stack is where execution contexts live. It follows **LIFO (Last In, First Out)**. Example: ```js function first() { second(); } function second() { console.log("Hello"); } first(); ``` Stack flow: ``` Global() → first() → second() ``` After `second()` finishes, it gets removed from the stack. If too many nested calls happen → stack overflow. --- ## Asynchronous Code & The Event Loop JavaScript is single-threaded. So how does this work? ```js setTimeout(() => { console.log("Timeout"); }, 0); console.log("Start"); ``` Output: ``` Start Timeout ``` Why? Because async functions go to the **Web APIs**, not directly into the stack. --- ## Microtasks vs Macrotasks ![Image](https://cdn.hashnode.com/res/hashnode/image/upload/v1668009168208/lgiS-UAil.png) There are two queues: ### Microtasks (Higher Priority) - Promises - queueMicrotask ### Macrotasks - setTimeout - setInterval - DOM events Example: ```js Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log("Promise")); setTimeout(() => console.log("Timeout"), 0); console.log("Start"); ``` Output: ``` Start Promise Timeout ``` Microtasks always execute before macrotasks. --- ## Final Mental Model Here is the full execution cycle simplified: ``` 1. Parse code 2. Create Global Execution Context 3. Memory Setup (Hoisting) 4. Execute code 5. Push/pop functions from Call Stack 6. Delegate async tasks 7. Event Loop processes: - Microtasks first - Macrotasks next ``` --- ## Why This Matters If you understand this cycle: - Hoisting becomes obvious - Closures become intuitive - Async behavior becomes predictable - Promise ordering makes sense JavaScript is not unpredictable. It is deterministic you just need the right mental model.
